questions.text: What is the primary function of IRDAI?
options: To sell insurance policies to the public
To protect the interests of policyholders and regulate the insurance industry
To invest insurance premiums in the stock market
questions.explanation: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) is the statutory body tasked with regulating and promoting the insurance industry and protecting policyholders.

questions.text: Which of the following risks is generally NOT insurable?
options: Speculative risk (like gambling or stock market losses)
questions.explanation: Insurance only covers 'Pure Risks' where there is a chance of loss or no loss. 'Speculative Risks' where there is a chance of profit are not insurable.

questions.text: What does a 'Super Top-up' Health Insurance policy do?
options: It provides unlimited coverage without any premium
It covers multiple claims whose aggregate value crosses the deductible limit in a policy year
It replaces the base health insurance policy
questions.explanation: A Super Top-up policy triggers when the total (aggregate) claims in a year cross a pre-defined deductible threshold, unlike a standard top-up which applies per claim.

questions.text: Under Section 80D of the Income Tax Act, what is the maximum tax deduction allowed for health insurance premium paid for self, spouse, and children (below 60 years)?
questions.explanation: For individuals below 60 years of age, the maximum tax deduction available under Section 80D is Rs. 25,000.

questions.text: Under Section 80D, what is the additional tax deduction limit for paying health insurance premiums for senior citizen parents (aged 60 and above)?
questions.explanation: An additional deduction of up to Rs. 50,000 is available for premiums paid for parents who are senior citizens.

questions.text: What is the Toll-Free number for the IRDAI Grievance Call Centre (IGCC)?
questions.explanation: Policyholders can register complaints against insurers by calling the IGCC toll-free number 155255 or 1800 4254 732.

questions.text: Which document constitutes the actual contract between the insurer and the policyholder?
questions.explanation: The Policy Document contains all the terms, conditions, exclusions, and warranties, serving as the legal contract.

questions.text: In health insurance, specific diseases like Cataract or Hernia usually have a waiting period of how long?
questions.explanation: Standard health policies have a 'Specific Disease Waiting Period' of usually 12 to 24 months for slow-growing ailments like cataract, hernia, or joint replacements.

questions.text: What is 'Bancassurance'?
options: An arrangement where a bank sells insurance products of an insurance company
Banks creating their own insurance companies
Insurance companies keeping their money in banks
questions.explanation: Bancassurance is a partnership between a bank and an insurance company where the bank uses its customer network to sell insurance products.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'Medical Examiner's Report (MER)'?
options: To treat a patient in the hospital
To assess the current health status of a proposer during underwriting
To provide a bill for claim reimbursement
questions.explanation: The MER is filled by a designated doctor during pre-policy medical check-ups to help the underwriter assess the exact health risk.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a 'Standard Age Proof'?
questions.explanation: Passports, School/Board passing certificates, PAN cards, and Birth Certificates are considered reliable and standard proofs of age.

questions.text: When can a policyholder request policy cancellation and get a refund on a pro-rata or short-period basis?
options: Any time during the policy year, provided no claim has been made
Only at the time of renewal
A policy can never be cancelled by the policyholder
questions.explanation: The insured can cancel the policy at any time. If no claims were made, the insurer refunds the premium for the remaining period based on short-period scales.

questions.text: Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY) provides a health cover of how much per family per year?
questions.explanation: PMJAY is a government scheme providing a cashless health cover of up to Rs. 5 Lakhs per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization.

questions.text: An agent has a 'Fiduciary Duty' towards the client. What does fiduciary mean?
options: Duty based on trust and acting in the best financial interest of the client
Duty to sell the most expensive policy
Duty to hide diseases from the insurer
questions.explanation: Fiduciary refers to a relationship built on trust and good faith, where the agent is legally and ethically bound to act in the client's best interest.

questions.text: Which of the following is typically a permanent exclusion in a health insurance policy?
options: Congenital External Anomalies (defects present from birth)
questions.explanation: External congenital anomalies (like a cleft lip) are standard permanent exclusions in retail health insurance.

questions.text: What is the maximum limit of an Ex-Gratia payment?
options: It is completely at the discretion of the Insurance Company as it is a goodwill gesture
questions.explanation: Ex-gratia is a payment made out of goodwill by the insurer when the claim is not strictly covered by policy terms, so there is no fixed limit.

questions.text: What does 'e-IA' stand for in insurance?
questions.explanation: An e-IA (Electronic Insurance Account) allows policyholders to hold all their insurance policies in an electronic/digital format in a single account.

questions.text: In case a claim form is submitted with all documents, normally within how many days should the reimbursement claim be paid?
questions.explanation: As per IRDAI guidelines, an insurer must settle or reject a claim within 30 days of receiving the last necessary document.

questions.text: What penalty does the insurance company pay if they delay the claim settlement beyond the stipulated time?
options: They pay double the claim amount
Interest at a rate 2% above the prevailing bank rate
questions.explanation: If the claim payment is delayed beyond 30 days, the insurer is liable to pay interest at 2% above the bank rate established by the RBI.

questions.text: Which network allows cash payment at a negotiated lower rate for non-cashless treatments in specific hospitals?
questions.explanation: PPN comprises hospitals where insurers/TPAs have agreed on specific package rates for procedures to control claims costs.

questions.text: For how long is a medical prescription for diagnostic tests generally considered valid by an insurer?
questions.explanation: Prescriptions for pre-hospitalization tests are typically accepted if they are within 30 days of the actual hospitalization.

questions.text: What does a 'Copayment' clause do to the health insurance premium?
options: It decreases the premium because the policyholder shares the risk
It has no effect on the premium
questions.explanation: Because the policyholder agrees to pay a fixed percentage of every claim, the insurer's liability decreases, which lowers the premium.

questions.text: If a person has a 10% co-payment clause and submits an approved claim of Rs. 1,00,000, how much will the insurance company pay?
questions.explanation: The insured pays 10% (Rs. 10,000) as co-payment, and the insurer pays the remaining 90% (Rs. 90,000).

questions.text: Who investigates major or complex insurance claims to assess the extent of loss?
options: A Surveyor / Loss Assessor or Field Investigator
questions.explanation: Surveyors or Field Investigators are assigned to physically verify complex, large, or suspicious claims by visiting the hospital or the patient's residence.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered 'Tertiary Healthcare'?
options: A specialized hospital performing complex surgeries like organ transplants
questions.explanation: Tertiary healthcare requires highly specialized equipment and expertise, such as neurosurgery, cancer management, or organ transplants.

questions.text: What is the condition called when a policy ceases to exist because the renewal premium was not paid even after the grace period?
questions.explanation: If the premium is not paid within the grace period (usually 30 days), the policy lapses and all accumulated benefits like waiting period waivers are lost.

questions.text: Is it mandatory for an insurance company to send a renewal notice to the policyholder?
options: No, it is a good practice but the primary responsibility to renew lies with the policyholder
Only if the sum insured is above 10 Lakhs
questions.explanation: While insurers usually send reminders, it is not a legal obligation. The policyholder must track the expiry date and renew on time.

questions.text: What happens in health insurance if the policyholder changes their occupation to a more hazardous one (like from an office worker to a mine worker)?
options: The policyholder must inform the insurer, who may alter the premium or terms
The policy automatically increases the sum insured
questions.explanation: A change in occupation to a hazardous one alters the physical hazard. The principle of utmost good faith requires informing the insurer.

questions.text: What is 'Reinsurance'?
options: Taking two policies from the same company
Insurance purchased by an insurance company from another insurance company to manage its risk
questions.explanation: Reinsurance is 'insurance for insurers'. Companies transfer portions of their risk portfolios to other parties to reduce the likelihood of paying a massive obligation.

questions.text: A customer is denied cashless facility by the TPA. Does this mean their claim is rejected?
options: No, they can still apply for reimbursement after paying the hospital bill themselves
Yes, and the policy is cancelled
No, the hospital will treat them for free
questions.explanation: Denial of cashless authorization is not a rejection of the claim. It just means the TPA needs more details, and the claim can be evaluated on a reimbursement basis later.

questions.text: What is the typical time limit for submitting a reimbursement claim form and documents after being discharged from the hospital?
options: Within 7 to 15 days (depending on the specific policy)
Any time before the policy expires
questions.explanation: Most standard policies require the complete set of original documents to be submitted within 15 days of discharge.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a valid ground for rejecting a claim under a health insurance policy?
options: Admission purely for diagnostic or investigation purposes without active treatment
Admission for an illness that is under a waiting period
The policyholder did not inform the agent about the hospitalization
Hiding a pre-existing disease at the time of taking the policy
questions.explanation: Informing the agent is not legally mandatory. The requirement is to inform the Insurer or TPA directly. Claims cannot be rejected just because the agent wasn't told.

questions.text: What is an 'Endorsement' in an insurance policy?
options: A written amendment or addition attached to the policy document changing its terms
The hospital's stamp on the bill
questions.explanation: Any change to the original policy (like a name correction, adding a family member, or address change) is executed via a document called an endorsement.

questions.text: Can a proposal form be filled by the insurance agent on behalf of the customer?
options: Yes, it is the agent's job to fill the form and the customer just signs
No, the proposer should ideally fill it. If the agent assists, the proposer must understand and sign the declaration
Only if the customer is illiterate
Yes, but the agent must use a pencil
questions.explanation: The proposer is responsible for the contents of the form. If an agent or third party fills it, they must read it back to the proposer, who must sign a declaration confirming they understood the details.

questions.text: If an insurance ombudsman passes a decision that the policyholder does not accept, what is the policyholder's next step?
options: They must accept it as the Ombudsman's decision is final for everyone
They can appeal to the IRDAI Chairman
They can approach consumer courts (Consumer Dispute Redressal Forums)
They can appeal to the Supreme Court immediately
questions.explanation: If the complainant rejects the Ombudsman's award, they retain the right to take the matter to a Consumer Court or regular civil court.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between a TPA and an Insurance Broker?
options: A Broker sells policies representing the customer; a TPA processes claims representing the insurer
A TPA sells policies; a Broker processes claims
A Broker is a government employee; a TPA is private
questions.explanation: Brokers are sales intermediaries who help clients buy insurance. TPAs are service intermediaries who help insurers manage and process health claims.

questions.text: Which pricing method applies a lower premium initially, which increases as the policyholder grows older in age brackets?
questions.explanation: In Age-banded pricing, premiums are fixed for specific age groups (e.g., 25-30, 31-35) and increase when the insured moves to the next age bracket.

questions.text: In the event of a dispute regarding a claim amount, if the dispute is strictly about the quantum (amount) and not liability, what mechanism is used?
questions.explanation: If the insurer accepts the liability to pay but there is a dispute over the exact amount, standard policy conditions require the matter to be referred to Arbitration.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered an 'Alternative Treatment' in India?
questions.explanation: In India, treatments under Ayurveda, Yoga/Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (AYUSH) are considered alternative treatments compared to conventional Allopathy.

questions.text: Which document is essential to prove that an accident was reported to the authorities in a medico-legal case (MLC) before processing a claim?
options: First Information Report (FIR) or Police Panchnama
questions.explanation: In accident cases involving road traffic or assault (Medico-Legal Cases), an FIR is mandatory to process the health or accident insurance claim.

questions.text: In an Employer-Employee Group Health Insurance policy, who is the 'Master Policyholder'?
questions.explanation: The Employer buys the policy, pays the premium, and holds the master contract, while employees are the beneficiaries who receive health cards.

questions.text: Tailor-made group policies can offer a benefit not usually found in retail individual policies. Which one?
options: Waiver of Pre-Existing Disease (PED) waiting period
Double claim for the same bill
questions.explanation: Large corporate group policies are often customized (tailor-made) to waive off the 4-year PED waiting period from day one.

questions.text: What is 'Proportionate Deduction' in the context of room rent caps?
options: Deducting money proportionally from the agent's commission
Reducing all associated medical charges (doctor fees, OT charges) in the same ratio if a higher category room is chosen
Refusing to pay the room rent entirely
questions.explanation: If a patient chooses a room more expensive than their policy limit allows, the insurer not only pays less for the room but also proportionately reduces associated costs like doctor's visits.

questions.text: What happens if a policyholder misplaces the original policy document?
options: The policyholder can apply for a duplicate policy document by paying a nominal fee and signing an indemnity bond
They can never make a claim again
They must buy a new policy
questions.explanation: A lost document does not cancel the contract. The insurer will issue a duplicate policy document upon request and submission of an indemnity bond.

questions.text: If a claim is found to be fraudulent, what action will the insurance company take?
options: Pay the claim but warn the customer
Repudiate (reject) the claim and cancel the policy with forfeiture of premium
Increase the premium for next year
Ask the TPA to pay half
questions.explanation: Fraud goes against the principle of Utmost Good Faith. The insurer has the right to completely reject the claim, cancel the policy, and keep the premium.

questions.text: Under the Consumer Protection Act, a complaint can be filed in the District Commission if the value of goods or services paid as consideration does not exceed?
options: Rs. 1 Crore (Updated CPA 2019 limits, revised to 50 Lakhs in 2021)
questions.explanation: As per the latest Consumer Protection (Jurisdiction of the District Commission, the State Commission and the National Commission) Rules, 2021, District Commissions have jurisdiction up to Rs. 50 Lakhs.

questions.text: Which entity provides 'Cashless Authorization' in a hospital?
options: The Third Party Administrator (TPA) or Insurer's internal claim team
questions.explanation: Authorization for cashless treatment is exclusively evaluated and provided by the TPA or the in-house claim processing team of the insurance company.

questions.text: A 'Morale Hazard' is best described as?
options: Deliberately burning down a building for insurance
Being careless about locking doors because 'I have insurance'
questions.explanation: Morale hazard is an attitude of indifference or carelessness towards loss because the person is insured. (Distinct from 'Moral' hazard which involves deliberate dishonest acts).

questions.text: What is the primary motive behind the principle of 'Proximate Cause'?
options: To find the most distant event that led to a loss
To identify the active, direct, and efficient cause that set in motion a chain of events leading to the loss
To find a reason to reject a claim
questions.explanation: Proximate cause helps insurers determine the actual, immediate, and efficient cause of a loss to check if that specific peril is covered under the policy.

questions.text: According to IRDAI, what is the maximum commission an agent can get for selling a standard retail health insurance policy in the first year?
questions.explanation: As per IRDAI regulations on remuneration, the maximum commission payable to an agent for retail health insurance is typically capped at 15%.
